rule_text: Before adjusting or customizing trading strategies, a trader must first master the fundamental technical foundations: edge, variance, and appropriate position sizing.
entry_conditions: Edge is quantified with statistical significance (p-value < 0.05 over minimum 100 trades)
Variance of strategy returns is measured and understood (drawdown limits defined)
Position sizing follows Kelly Criterion with 25% fractional adjustment or fixed percentage risk (max 1% per trade)
exit_conditions: If any fundamental criterion drops below threshold, revert to base strategy until revalidated

rule_text: Before adjusting or customizing trading strategies, a trader must first master the fundamental technical foundations: edge, variance, and appropriate position sizing.

Variance is documented through max drawdown and Sharpe ratio measurement
Position sizing uses fixed fractional method (2-3% risk per trade) or simplified Kelly
exit_conditions: Adapt strategy parameters within 20% variance from base if edge remains positive

rule_text: Before adjusting or customizing trading strategies, a trader must first master the fundamental technical foundations: edge, variance, and appropriate position sizing.

Position sizing follows fixed percentage rule (3-5% risk per trade)
exit_conditions: Customize and adapt strategy rapidly; revert only if drawdown exceeds 30%

rule_text: Before entering any trade, a trader must quantify both the level of risk being incurred and the amount of edge expected, using mathematical measurement rather than relying solely on intuition or market folklore.
entry_conditions: Expected edge (measured as win rate × avg win - loss rate × avg loss) ≥ 2.0% per trade
Risk-to-reward ratio ≤ 1:3
Probability of edge occurring < 5% by chance (statistical significance test required)
Maximum position risk ≤ 0.5% of account
exit_conditions: Exit if measured edge deteriorates below 1.5%
Exit if risk exposure exceeds 0.75% of account

rule_text: A trader should only include trading methods and risk controls in their system if they can be measured and quantified; measurability is a prerequisite for management and edge evaluation.
entry_conditions: Signal has been backtested over minimum 500 trades
Signal shows measurable positive edge with p-value < 0.05

exit_conditions: Live performance diverges from backtest by more than 50%
Measurement quality degrades or data becomes unreliable

rule_text: A trader should identify and trade discrepancies between their own volatility forecast and the option market's implied volatility estimate, as this is the largest source of edge in option trading.
entry_conditions: Measured historical volatility (using Yang-Zhang method) calculated over 60-day lookback
Implied volatility from options market exceeds measured volatility by ≥2.0 standard deviations
Confirmation: At least 2 of 3 alternative methods (Parkinson, Rogers-Satchell, Garman-Klass) agree directionally with Yang-Zhang
exit_conditions: Implied volatility converges to measured volatility within 1.0 std dev
Time decay reaches 7 days to expiration
Measured volatility moves beyond initial forecast by >1.5 std devs (forecast invalidation)

source_citation: Options Trading, Euan Sinclair, Acknowledgments, pp. 6-16
raw_chunk_text:
Although our focus is to look for situations where implied volatility is
at variance with our forecast of realized volatility, dynamics of the implied
volatility surface are also interesting and impor
rule_text: A trader should monitor and understand the dynamics of the implied volatility surface across strikes and time to optimize trade execution and timing, particularly when trading volatility forecasts.
entry_conditions: IV surface skewness within 1 standard deviation of 60-day historical mean
Term structure slope (30-day vs 90-day IV) within normal range (±0.5 vega points)
Strike dispersion (ATM vs 25-delta strangle IV difference) matches historical median ±25%
Implied volatility forecast confidence > 70%
exit_conditions: Surface dynamics revert to historical norms
Any dimension breaches 1.5 standard deviations

rule_text: A trader should monitor and understand the dynamics of the implied volatility surface across strikes and time to optimize trade execution and timing, particularly when trading volatility forecasts.
entry_conditions: IV surface skewness deviates >2 standard deviations from 30-day historical mean OR crossing regime threshold
Term structure slope exhibits sharp inflection (>1.5 vega point move in <5 days)
Strike dispersion variance >50% from historical median (indicating extreme tail hedging or buyback)
Implied volatility forecast confidence > 40%
exit_conditions: Position reaches 150% of forecasted profit target
Surface normalizes to within 1 standard deviation of mean
Time decay advantage exhausted (>70% of theta collected)

source_citation: Options Trading by Euan Sinclair, Acknowledgments, pp. 6-16
raw_chunk_text:
To profit from our forecast of volatility we need to hedge, so that our
risk is actually realized volatility. Hedging removes the risks that we do not
wish to take. We wish to accept risks that we bel
rule_text: A trader should hedge unwanted risks (drift and interest rate exposure) while retaining exposure to misprice volatility, adjusting hedge frequency based on the cost-benefit tradeoff between risk reduction and hedging expense.
entry_conditions: Volatility forecast identified and position established
Cumulative unhedged delta exposure exceeds 20% of position notional
Cumulative unhedged vega exposure exists

parameters.interest_rate_sensitivity_threshold: hedge if DV01 > 0.5% of position value

exit_conditions: Rehedge when delta exceeds ±20% of position notional
Exit underlying position when volatility forecast invalidated

source_citation: Options Trading, Euan Sinclair, Ch.8, pp.17-65
raw_chunk_text:
Chapter 8 demonstrates how different choices for trade sizing can
dramatically affect returns. We introduce Kelly betting and compare it
with other schemes, such as fixed-sized trading and proportiona
rule_text: A trader should calibrate position sizing methodology based on edge characteristics and risk tolerance, as sizing decisions materially affect returns, drawdowns, and risk of ruin.
entry_conditions: Trade has positive expected value
Risk of ruin calculation is < 1% over 100 trades
exit_conditions: Stop loss at 2x ATR from entry
Profit target at 1x risk:reward ratio

rule_text: A trader should maintain systematic post-trade record-keeping and analysis across multiple performance metrics to distinguish genuine edge from chance and enable continuous improvement.
entry_conditions: Strategy has generated at least 100 completed trades
Win rate >= 50% AND Sharpe ratio >= 1.0
Maximum drawdown <= 15% of account
Calmar ratio >= 1.5
exit_conditions: Any metric falls below threshold for 20 consecutive trades
Omega ratio < 1.0 for trailing 50-trade window

rule_text: A trader should maintain systematic post-trade record-keeping and analysis across multiple performance metrics to distinguish genuine edge from chance and enable continuous improvement.

source_citation: Options Trading, Euan Sinclair, Ch.8, p.17-65
raw_chunk_text:
Shorting a stock is a privilege a broker extends to customers and this
generally needs to be paid for. This can be accounted for synthetically by
assuming a fake dividend yield to reflect these costs.
rule_text: A trader should adjust the cost-of-carry rate used in option pricing models to reflect the actual financing and carrying costs of the underlying asset, which vary by asset class and market structure.
entry_conditions: Underlying is a stock with documented borrow cost > 0
Calculate implied cost-of-carry: r + q* + borrow_fee_high
exit_conditions: Exit when borrow cost declines below assumed rate
Exit if position delta diverges from hedge ratio by > 10%

rule_text: A trader should compare the risk-reward of selling implied volatility across multiple underlyings by evaluating the spread between implied and realized volatility in historical context, and prioritize selling where the premium is most attractive relative to both the individual asset's volatility cone and the current overall market regime.

Overall market volatility regime is not in extreme tail (VIX < 85th percentile equivalent)

Asset underperforms peers by 2%+
Overall market regime shifts to extreme

rule_text: A trader should exploit elevated option bid-ask spreads by selling volatility when market conditions show risk aversion, negative skewness, and fat tails in the physical return distribution, as these conditions cause market makers to widen spreads defensively.

VIX or implied volatility percentile > 75th (risk aversion signal)
Bid-ask spread > 90th percentile of trailing 60-day spreads
exit_conditions: Skewness reverts to > -0.3
Kurtosis falls below 0.5
Bid-ask spread compresses below 60th percentile

rule_text: A trader should identify and trade based on the detected regime of implied volatility, as VIX exhibits distinct mean-reverting behavior within two separate regimes (high-volatile 20-40 and quiet 10-20), with understanding that mean reversion behavior is locally persistent rather than globally constant.
entry_conditions: Identify current VIX regime: HIGH (20-40) or QUIET (10-20)
VIX deviates beyond 75th percentile of current regime range
Confirm regime persistence over prior 20 trading days
Position for reversion to regime midpoint (30 for HIGH, 15 for QUIET)
exit_conditions: Exit when VIX reaches regime midpoint or opposite percentile threshold
Exit if regime switches (persistence breaks)

7 days before earnings announcement
Implied volatility below 52-week percentile 50th
Bid/ask spread ≤ 1 cent
exit_conditions: Close of day before earnings announcement
Stop loss: -15% of straddle entry cost (tighter)

rule_text: A trader should buy delta-neutral straddles before scheduled economic news events when implied volatility is depressed, as wide analyst dispersion creates asymmetric payoff potential from the upcoming volatility expansion.
entry_conditions: IV percentile < 25th (significantly depressed)
Days to scheduled economic news = 5 calendar days (Monday close before Friday release)
Position size limited to 1-2% account risk
Delta-neutral straddle (ATM or near ATM)
exit_conditions: At scheduled news release time (hard exit)
Close of trading day before announcement (max hold period)

rule_text: A trader should isolate and quantify event-driven volatility by decomposing implied volatility term structure, then size positions based on the magnitude of event volatility relative to forward volatility.
entry_conditions: σ₁ (front-month IV) > σ₂ (second-month IV)
σₑ (event volatility) > 0.30 × σ₁ (event vol > 30% of front-month IV)
Forward volatility σf = √[T₂²σ₂² - T₁²σ₁²] / (T₂ - T₁) calculated and validated
exit_conditions: Exit at σₑ becoming negative or σ₁ < σ₂ (term structure normalizes)
Exit if event volatility contracts below 20% of initial measured value

rule_text: Construct a delta-neutral spread using gamma-weighted legs positioned against an unfavorable implied volatility skew (downward sloping) to capture positive theta while realized variance cancels, creating a model-independent arbitrage opportunity.
entry_conditions: Implied volatility slope (OTM strike IV - ATM strike IV) < -3% (steep downward slope)
Realized volatility (20-day rolling) < 50th percentile of 1-year history
Delta neutrality achieved: |delta_long_leg + delta_short_leg| < 0.05
Gamma ratio weighting: short_leg_quantity = gamma_ATM / gamma_OTM with tolerance ±5%
exit_conditions: IV slope flattens above -1.5%
Position delta drifts beyond ±0.10
Theta decay < 50% of expected daily theta
15 days to expiration or position P&L > +3%

rule_text: A trader should construct delta-neutral risk-reversals (short OTM put, long OTM call) and dynamically hedge them because sticky delta assumptions create vega asymmetry that generates profits regardless of underlying direction.
entry_conditions: Construct delta-neutral risk reversal with delta = 0.00 ± 0.02
Call vega exceeds put vega by at least 15% when underlying at entry level
Select strikes where call is 1.5 standard deviations OTM and put is 1.5 standard deviations OTM
Confirm sticky delta regime is in effect (historical vol smile stable)
exit_conditions: Exit when delta drifts beyond ±0.05
Close position if vega asymmetry collapses below 10%
Take profit at 20% of max theoretical profit

rule_text: A trader should calibrate the volatility surface gradient (backbone slope) by regressing historical implied volatility changes against underlying price movements, rather than using a static sticky-delta assumption, to better capture the empirical relationship between price drops and volatility increases.
entry_conditions: Underlying price movement detected
Regression slope parameter estimated from 252+ days of historical data
At-the-money implied volatility adjusted by: IV_new = IV_old + (slope × underlying_return), where slope is bottom quartile of regression estimate
exit_conditions: Revert to sticky-delta if regression R-squared falls below 0.15

Stop loss if deviation increases to 1.5 std in wrong direction

rule_text: A trader should reject or reprice option trades when the cumulative cost of delta hedging erodes edge below acceptable thresholds, adjusting the implied volatility ask price upward to compensate for transaction costs.

exit_conditions: Do not enter if repricing condition is not met

rule_text: A trader should distinguish between volatility (market risk) and transaction costs (bid/ask spread) as separate phenomena, and adjust hedging frequency based on which cost dominates, rather than treating them as equivalent.
entry_conditions: Estimate transaction cost (bid/ask spread + commissions)
Estimate realized volatility over next rehedge period
Compare: realized_volatility / transaction_cost ratio
Hedge only if ratio > 3.0

parameters.spread_tolerance: only rehedge if spread < 1 std dev of recent spreads

exit_conditions: Exit hedge if spread widens beyond 2x recent average
Reduce position if vol/cost ratio falls below 2.5

rule_text: When selling volatility/insurance via options, do not cap risk estimates using only historical extremes; price in tail risk for events that have never occurred, since the premium you collect includes compensation for unprecedented events.

Apply tail-risk multiplier to derive stress-test worst case
exit_conditions: Position loss exceeds stress-adjusted worst case by 50%

Estimated transaction costs of individual hedges > 2bps of notional
exit_conditions: Portfolio delta < 2 deltas
Correlation regime breaks below 0.70
Time decay decay falls below position gamma benefit

rule_text: A trader should combine dynamic hedging with static hedging using options to offset jump risks that dynamic hedging alone cannot manage, especially when aggregating positions across multiple underlyings.
entry_conditions: Position vega accumulated across 2+ underlyings
Static hedge ratio = 80% of total vega exposure
exit_conditions: Exit when static hedge vega ratio falls below 0.70 of current exposure
Rebalance when any single underlying vega exceeds 30% of portfolio vega

source_citation: Options Trading, Euan Sinclair, Ch.6, p.108-143, Figure 7.5
rule_text: Hedging frequency inversely reduces P/L dispersion in delta-hedged options positions, following a mathematical relationship where dispersion scales with N^(-1/2), where N is the number of hedges.
entry_conditions: Initiating a delta-hedged short vega position (short straddle, strangle, or short call/put spread)
Daily or intraday volatility realization is expected
exit_conditions: Hedge N times where N >= 20 (reducing dispersion by ~78%)
Exit when realized vol deviates significantly from expected

source_citation: Options Trading, Euan Sinclair, Ch.6, p.108-143, Figure 7.5
rule_text: Hedging frequency inversely reduces P/L dispersion in delta-hedged options positions, following a mathematical relationship where dispersion scales with N^(-1/2), where N is the number of hedges.
entry_conditions: Initiating a delta-hedged option position (long or short vega)
Volatility environment is expected to persist for multiple weeks
exit_conditions: Hedge N times where N = 10 (reducing dispersion by ~68%)
Reassess position if realized vol moves outside 1 standard deviation band

source_citation: Options Trading, Euan Sinclair, Ch.6, p.108-143, Figure 7.5
rule_text: Hedging frequency inversely reduces P/L dispersion in delta-hedged options positions, following a mathematical relationship where dispersion scales with N^(-1/2), where N is the number of hedges.
entry_conditions: Initiating a delta-hedged option position where transaction costs are material
Expected realized volatility is stable and close to implied volatility
exit_conditions: Hedge N times where N = 4 (reducing dispersion by ~50%)
Exit if realized vol exceeds 2 standard deviations from forecast

rule_text: A trader should increase hedging frequency when holding positions exposed to volatility risk, because realized volatility can differ from expected volatility and cause losses despite correct directional assessment, though this benefit diminishes in the presence of jump risk.
entry_conditions: Position established in options or leveraged equity
Estimated realized volatility differs from implied by >0.5%
Underlying has low jump probability (equity-like, not commodity futures)
exit_conditions: Position closed
Time decay makes rehedging cost exceed expected benefit

rule_text: Adjust hedging frequency based on market regime: reduce hedging frequency in trending markets when long gamma (use higher volatility estimates), increase hedging frequency in choppy/trendless markets (use lower volatility estimates).
entry_conditions: Position is long gamma (long options or spreads)

Regime shifts from trending to choppy (SMA slope inverts)

Next we more generally examine the problem of choosing a hedging
volatility. We assume we sell an option at an implied volatility, 0,, and
we then hedge at the realized volatility, o,. Let’s examine h
rule_text: A trader should bias hedging volatility upward when holding short gamma in range-bound markets and downward when holding short gamma in trending markets; conversely, bias downward for long gamma in range-bound markets and upward for long gamma in trending markets.
entry_conditions: Identify gamma position (long or short)
Confirm market regime with 20-period ATR or similar (trending if ATR > 1.5 * 50-period avg; ranging if ATR < 0.8 * 50-period avg)
Apply minimal volatility bias adjustment (±5% from realized vol estimate)
exit_conditions: Exit when regime changes (trending to ranging or vice versa)
Exit when gamma exposure reverses
Exit when realized volatility moves within ±3% of implied volatility

Minimum 45 days to expiration
exit_conditions: IV drops below 1.25x realized volatility
Position reaches 50% max profit
15 days to expiration or underlying moves within 1.5 std devs of strike

rule_text: A trader should construct volatility positions across a range of strikes rather than concentrating at a single strike, because gamma exposure (and thus volatility profit potential) is distributed across the underlying's path, and different strikes capture volatility risk differently based on their distance from at-the-money.
entry_conditions: Volatility forecast established and thesis is directionally agnostic
Implied volatility is below realized or expected volatility
Select strikes within 0.5 standard deviations of current ATM
exit_conditions: Close position at 21 DTE to capture maximum gamma before time decay accelerates
Exit if underlying moves beyond 1.0 stdev of entry point

rule_text: A trader should avoid hedging long option positions when realized volatility is high, and only attempt hedging when realized volatility has fallen to low levels—but recognize this creates a paradox: low realized volatility means the volatility forecast was wrong, and hedging at that point may lock in losses from gamma compression by market makers.
entry_conditions: Realized volatility (20-day window) < 60% of initial implied volatility
Position is long gamma (long option buyer position)
Underlying price near long strike (within 1 standard deviation)
exit_conditions: Close or significantly reduce position rather than hedge
If must hedge: use minimal delta-neutral rehedge only

rule_text: Position sizing must be determined by a systematic rule based on trade quality, not ad hoc adjustment based on trader intuition or 'feel'.
entry_conditions: Trade setup identified via systematic edge metric
Position size pre-determined by rule before entry

parameters.sizing_rule: Fixed % of bankroll or fixed dollar amount per trade regardless of perceived quality

rule_text: A trader should reduce position sizing to a fraction of the Kelly criterion when probability estimation uncertainty is high, to protect against overestimation of edge while accepting lower expected returns in exchange for lower volatility and drawdown risk.
entry_conditions: Estimated edge exists (win_probability > 50% OR risk_reward > 1:1)
Confidence in probability estimate is below 75th percentile
No recent string of losses indicating possible overestimation
exit_conditions: Stop loss at 2% of account
Take profit at 1.5x risk risked
Exit if confidence estimate drops below 60%

rule_text: When parameter uncertainty exists (measured by posterior distribution variance), reduce position sizing below the Kelly ratio proportionally to the probability that the true win rate may be below breakeven.
entry_conditions: Win/loss history sufficient to estimate posterior distribution (N >= 20 observations)
Calculate posterior standard deviation of win probability using formula: sqrt((w(N-w+2)) / ((N+2)^2(N+3)))
If StdDev > 0.12, apply multiplicative Kelly discount of (1 - StdDev(p)); else use full Kelly
exit_conditions: Exit when cumulative loss reaches kelly_reduction_factor * account_size
Recalculate posterior distribution every 10 trades; resume full Kelly when StdDev drops below 0.12

rule_text: When multiple trading opportunities with different payoff ratios exist on the same event, allocate capital across them jointly using Kelly optimization rather than sizing each position independently, because the optimal total allocation differs from the sum of individual optimal allocations.
entry_conditions: Two or more tradeable opportunities identified on same underlying event
Win probability and payoff ratios quantified for each opportunity
Opportunities are positively correlated (same event driver)
exit_conditions: Event resolved or correlation assumption broken
Bankroll drawdown exceeds 15%

rule_text: A trader should evaluate and potentially close or adjust existing positions when market repricing makes the current position economically inferior to alternative market instruments offering better terms, because marked-to-market P/L is what matters operationally—not unrealized thesis value.

Close position and redeploy capital at market terms

Time decay reduces option premium by 50%

Time decay: 14 days to expiration

Each of these ETNs depends not only on the level of the VIX but also

expensive than the first then VXX loses to the rolling
rule_text: A trader should avoid or short VXX when the VIX futures term structure is in contango (upward sloping), because the rolling process causes daily decay proportional to the slope between front and second contracts.
entry_conditions: VIX futures term structure is in contango (Month2_Future > Month1_Future)
Contango spread (Month2 - Month1) > 2.0 points
Expected daily decay rate > 0.067 (2.0 / 30 days)
exit_conditions: Contango spread collapses below 1.0 point
Term structure inverts to backwardation

source_citation: Options Trading, Euan Sinclair, Ch. 14, pp. 250–298 (AAPL trade example, Fig. 14.7)
rule_text: A trader should execute volatility-selling trades when implied volatility significantly exceeds the expected realized volatility over the option's lifetime, accepting short-term drawdowns because profit derives from the volatility differential over time, not daily price moves.

Initial vega exposure is at least $1,000 per contract
Expected profit from volatility differential = vega × (implied_vol − forecast_realized_vol) ≥ 2× max acceptable daily loss
exit_conditions: Cumulative loss exceeds 50% of initial expected vega profit
Implied volatility converges to within 5 points of realized volatility forecast
Time decay has captured 70% of initial vega value

14 days to expiration (whichever comes first)

rule_text: A trader must maintain comprehensive, accurate records of all trade P&L to enable statistical improvement and consistent profitability through informed sizing decisions.

rule_text: A trader should reduce position sizing when market noise exhibits fat tails (excess kurtosis), because optimal sizing formulas calibrated to normal distributions become too aggressive under non-normal conditions.
entry_conditions: Asset price deviates from fair value by N standard deviations (where N is calibrated entry threshold)
Kurtosis detection: Rolling 20-period excess kurtosis > 1.0 (moderate fat tails detected)
Commit initial position sizing at 25% of optimal formula result
exit_conditions: Asset returns to fair value (mean reversion exit)
Stop loss if position moves 3 standard deviations against entry
